coredata.dc:title: Lanthanide coordination polymers as luminescent laccase mimics for ratiometric sensing of dopamine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ruan, Chen
Zhou, Siqi
Wu, Xinru
Zou, Lina
Wang, Rong
Li, Gaiping
coredata.dc:description: Some metal ions, with inner enzyme-like catalytic activity, could be doped into lanthanide coordination polymers (Ln CPs) through coordination, which has been proved as a facile strategy to prepare the luminescent nanozymes. In this study, Cu-doped Ln CPs with laccase-mimic activity and double luminescence were rationally designed and synthesized by self-assembly of guanine monophosphate (GMP), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA), Cu2+ and Tb3+ in buffer solution at room temperature. The obtained probes Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs not only emitted green fluorescence of Tb3+ and blue fluorescence of ATA simultaneously under irradiation at the same wavelength, but also processed enhanced laccase-like activity for catalyzing the oxidation of phenolic substrates. Upon dopamine (DA), the probes catalyzed the oxidation of DA to polydopamine (PDA), which effectively quenched the fluorescence of Tb3+ due to the internal filtration effect. Based on this, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for DA was constructed accordingly, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity ratio of Tb3+ to ATA (F547/F427) was linearly correlated with the DA concentration in the range of 1 to 400 μM, with a detection limit of 0.44 μM. Besides, this sensor could be used to detect DA in human serum samples with good recovery, which results were highly consistent with that of HPLC method. The constituent and luminescence tunability, as well as the extraordinarily facile synthesis, made Ln CPs a potential platform for designing and preparing the integrated multifunctional probe for special target in sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide coordination polymers
Laccase mimics
Dopamine
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and Characterization of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs
Laccase-like activity of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs and their response toward DA
Analysis and determination of DA in serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectra of ATA, Tb-GMP, Tb/Cu-GMP and Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs. (B) Fluorescence profiles of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs prepared with different ratio of Tb3+ to GMP.

SEM images of (A) Tb-GMP/ATA CPs, (B) Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs (Illustrations were pictures of CPs). (C) XRD patterns of Tb-GMP/ATA CPs (a), Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs (b). (D) FT-IR spectra of GMP (purple line), Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs (blue line), and ATA (pink line). (E) Enlarged FT-IR spectra of GMP (purple line), Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs (blue line), and ATA (pink line).

(A) Schematic representation of the 2, 4-DP and 4-AP reactions catalyzed by Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs and Tb-GMP/ATA CPs. (B) UV–vis spectra of various reaction system. (C) (a) Differential absorption of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs + DA (30 min) and Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs + DA (0 min), and fluorescence spectra of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs (b) (Inset: 200 μM of DA before and after oxidation with Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs).
(A) Fluorescence spectra of Tb-GMP/ATA CPs and Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs without and with DA, (B) Fluorescence spectra of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs, Tb-GMP/ATA CPs + Cu2+ + DA and Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs + DA.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Tb-GMP CPs and ATA. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs at different excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence intensity of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs at 427 nm and 547 nm (C) as Tb3+ to Cu2+ ratio with and without DA; (D) in Tris-HCl buffer solution as a function of pH value; (E) with and without DA (200 μM) versue pH value, and (F) in different buffer solution at pH 8. (G) Fluorescence intensity ratio (I547/I427) of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs varies with time after addition of DA.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs in Tris-HCl at pH 8 with the addition of different concentrations of DA. (B) Fluorescence intensity ratio of the system I427/I547 as a function of DA concentration from 1-400 μM.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs in Tris-HCl at pH 7.4 with the addition of different concentrations of DA. (B) Fluorescence intensity ratio of the system I427/I547 as a function of DA concentration from 100-1200 μM.
Selectivity of the Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs sensor for DA.
Schematic representation of the fluorescence determination strategy for DA.
Analysis of DA in serum samples (n = 3) by this sensor.
Analysis of DA in serum samples (n = 3) by HPLC.

head.ce:title: Lanthanide coordination polymers as luminescent laccase mimics for ratiometric sensing of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Some metal ions, with inner enzyme-like catalytic activity, could be doped into lanthanide coordination polymers (Ln CPs) through coordination, which has been proved as a facile strategy to prepare the luminescent nanozymes. In this study, Cu-doped Ln CPs with laccase-mimic activity and double luminescence were rationally designed and synthesized by self-assembly of guanine monophosphate (GMP), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA), Cu2+ and Tb3+ in buffer solution at room temperature. The obtained probes Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs not only emitted green fluorescence of Tb3+ and blue fluorescence of ATA simultaneously under irradiation at the same wavelength, but also processed enhanced laccase-like activity for catalyzing the oxidation of phenolic substrates. Upon dopamine (DA), the probes catalyzed the oxidation of DA to polydopamine (PDA), which effectively quenched the fluorescence of Tb3+ due to the internal filtration effect. Based on this, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for DA was constructed accordingly, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity ratio of Tb3+ to ATA (F547/F427) was linearly correlated with the DA concentration in the range of 1 to 400 μM, with a detection limit of 0.44 μM. Besides, this sensor could be used to detect DA in human serum samples with good recovery, which results were highly consistent with that of HPLC method. The constituent and luminescence tunability, as well as the extraordinarily facile synthesis, made Ln CPs a potential platform for designing and preparing the integrated multifunctional probe for special target in sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a neurotransmitter that plays an irreplaceable role in maintaining various physiological processes in the brain [1,2]. Abnormal DA levels could lead to many neurological disorders such as Parkinson, Alzheimer, Schizophrenia, Huntington and Aprosexia [3,4]. Therefore, developing sensitive DA assays is of great significance for human health, and a general strategy is relying on the catalytic oxidation of DA by laccase to black products [5]. Nevertheless, natural laccase has some limitations on its practical application, such as high price and poor stability [3,6]. In contrast, artificial laccase with higher stability and lower cost is more appealing to researchers [7].

Laccase is a multicopper oxidase produced in organisms such as bacteria, fungi, and plants, and its center of catalytic activity is derived from four copper sites [8,9]. During the oxidation process, laccase reduces molecular oxygen to water without producing or requiring H2O2, making it a green catalyst [10–12]. Currently, some novel Cu-based materials with laccase-mimicking activity have been reported and widely explored for the colorimetric method in the field of DA assay [13,14]. For example, Liang et al. offered an exceptionally simple yet functional Cu MOFs with laccase-like activity based on guanine monophosphate (GMP) coordinated copper (Ⅱ) ions [15]. GMP is a highly flexible biological ligand with desirable water solubility, good biocompatibility and environmental friendliness, which could spontaneously coordinate with many kinds of metal ions. Besides, it is also an efficient antenna molecule to sensitize Tb3+ luminescence [16,17]. In recent years, nucleotide-based lanthanide infinite coordination polymers (Ln CPs) with excellent fluorescence properties have attracted extensive attention and have been successfully applied in the fields of chemical sensing and catalysis [18–20]. In addition, due to the self-adaptive inclusiveness granted by its structural flexibility, one or more different tailor-made guests could be accommodated, which helped in designing polymers for various capabilities [21–23]. Meanwhile, some metal ions, such as Cu2+, Fe2+, and Ce4+, could be simultaneously doped into the Ln CPs and endow them with enzyme-mimicking properties [24–26], which may open up new possibilities for DA sensing through combination of fluorescence analysis with nanozymes. Generally, the obtained polymers exhibited obviously enhanced catalytic activities compared to that of free metal ions [27,28]. Additionally, most reported fluorescent methods relied on the changes of single fluorescence intensity, which is susceptible to the fluctuations originated from environment and instrument. Ratiometric analysis can largely eliminate the interference by using the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths as the read-out signal, and thus improve the stability and accuracy of the measurement [29,30]. Therefore, it is reasonable to design a luminescent laccase-like Ln CPs by doping Cu2+ and ATA, and construct a ratiometric platform for DA assay with high accuracy by taking advantage of the inner calibration, which has remained unexplored before.

Herein, the multifunctional nanoprobes by selecting Tb3+ and Cu2+ as central metal ions, GMP and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) as bridging ligands were designed. They self-assembled spontaneously in the buffer solution under stirring, and the obtained Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs emitted green fluorescence of Tb3+ due to the antenna effect of GMP. Blue-emitting ATA acted as auxiliary ligand offering the reference fluorescent signal at different positions from that of Tb3+ for ratio assay. The probes could catalyze the DA oxidation with the dissolved O2 based on the laccase-like activity, and the black product PDA quenched the fluorescence of Tb3+ due to the internal filtration effect.Based on this strategy, a ratiometric DA assay was realized Scheme 1 .

Dopamine hydrochloride (DA) was obtained from Macklin Chemical. Tetraterbium nitrate pentahydrate (Tb(NO3)3⋅5H2O), copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O), aspartic acid (Asp), calcium chloride (CaCl2), acetylcholine (ACH), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), carbamide (urea), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), glycine (Gly), glutathione (GSH), glutamic acid (Glu) and cysteine (Cys) were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N’-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), guanine monophosphate (GMP) and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris) and 2-morpholineethanesulfonic acid (MES) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Human blood serum (HBS) samples were obtained from healthy volunteers and stored at 4 °C before use. All of the chemicals were of at least analytical grade and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were used throughout the study with Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ·cm−1). What’s more, all experiments were carried out at room temperature.

UV–vis spectra were obtained using a Fluorescence and Absorbance Spectrometer Duetta (Horiba Science, Canada). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on F97 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Prism Technology Co., Ltd. Shanghai, China). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (ZEISS Sigma 300) was used to characterize the morphologies. The infrared spectrum was detected by Nicolet Is50 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Co., Ltd., USA). Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.1 M) was adjusted to different pH values through HCl solution (10 M) with a FE28 digital pH meter (Mettler-Toledo, Shanghai, China). HPLC-UV (Agilent 1260 Infinity II).

Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs were synthesized according to a self-assembly of GMP, ATA and metal ions (Cu2+ and Tb3+) in Tris-HCl (0.1 M pH 7.4) buffer solution. Initially, 1 mL of GMP aqueous solution (14 mM) and 1 mL of ATA aqueous solution (1 mM) were added to a mixture containing 1 mL of Tb(NO3)3 aqueous solution (10 mM) and 1 mL of CuCl2·2H2O (1 mM). Immediately, the color of the solution was changed from white to green suspension. Then, the resulting mixture was vigorously shaken and incubated for 3 h at room temperature to form Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs. The product was collected by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 5 min and washed three times with ultrapure water. Finally, the collected CPs were dispersed in water and stored in a refrigerator at 4℃ for further use. Tb/Cu-GMP CPs and Tb-GMP/ATA CPs were prepared using the similar procedure as described above in the absence of ATA and Cu2+, respectively. Similarly, Tb-GMP CPs were synthesized without addition of Cu2+ and ATA.

For DA detection, 50 μL Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs and 150 μL Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.1 M pH 8) were mixed with DA at different concentrations and the mixture was well shaken with a final volume of 300 μL. The fluorescence emission spectrum in the range of 370–580 nm was recorded under excitation wavelength of 289 nm.

The same test procedure described above were used to estimate interference from substances including KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, ASP, Cys, Glu, GSH, ACH, Gly and urea. The final concentration of DA was 380 μM, ASP, Gly and Cys were 5 times higher than DA concentration, GSH concentration was 380 μM, NE and EP concentration were 38 μM, and the rest were 10 times higher than DA concentration.

Human serum was selected as the real sample to evaluate the practicability of this assay. Some pre-treatments were carried out to remove impurities. Firstly, acetonitrile was added to the blood sample (the volume ratio of acetonitrile and blood was 1.5:1), followed by shaking for two minutes, and then the product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the proteins. Secondly, the obtained serum was diluted 100-fold with Tris-HCl buffer solution, and then different amount of DA were added to prepare the spiked serum samples. Finally, the sample was added to 50 μL of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs, and Tris-HCl buffer (100 mM, pH 8) was added to keep the final volume of the mixture at 300 μL for assay.

Tb-GMP CPs are excellent luminescence materials benefiting from the superior luminescence, facile synthesis, and component adjustability [22]. Cu2+ with inherent enzymatic-like activity could be easily introduced through coordination for preparation luminescent laccase mimics [15]. Besides, blue-emitting ATA serving as auxiliary ligand to provide the reference fluorescent signal for the ratio assay. It could be efficiently excited together with Tb3+ under a single excitation wavelength, and revealed a spectral distinguishable fluorescence peak [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and Characterization of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs
ce:section.ce:para: Tb3+ has the advantages of large Stokes shift, narrow emission band and long fluorescence lifetime. However, due to the low absorption cross section and the non-radioactive inactivation of terbium ions by the O–H vibrations of coordinated water molecules, the luminous efficiency of Tb3+ alone is lower. GMP, a biocompatible nucleotide, can spontaneously coordinate with Tb3+ to form CPs, and serve as effective antenna molecules to sensitize its luminescence [16,22]. It could be clearly seen from Fig. 1 A that Tb-GMP CPs displayed two narrow fluorescence emission peaks at 491 nm and 547 nm, corresponding to the characteristic luminescence of Tb3+. When Cu2+ was doped, the fluorescence emission of Tb3+ decreased significantly, which is consistent with the previous reports that Cu2+ could interact with the bases of GMP and then quench the fluorescence of Tb3+ by electron or energy transfer [32]. When another luminescent center ATA was introduced, as expected, dual-luminescent Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs were obtained. They emitted both fluorescence of ATA and Tb3+, which were distinguishable and could be efficiently excited at the same excitation wavelength. In the meantime, the luminescence of Tb3+ increased to some extent compared with that of Tb/Cu-GMP CPs. The luminescence intensity of the probe is highly related to the content of ligands. Therefore, several CPs were prepared by adding various concentration of GMP in the synthesis while keeping other conditions constant, and their corresponding fluorescence were determined (Fig. 1B). For the strongest luminescence intensities of the probes, the ratio of Tb3+ to GMP of 5:7 was selected in the follow-up experiments.

The precipitates of Tb-GMP/ATA CPs were milky-white, while that of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs were pale green, demonstrating that Cu2+ had been successfully doped into the CPs. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to characterize their morphologies. As shown, both of them revealed aggregated nanoparticles with a network structure (Fig. 2 A and B). Meanwhile, as shown in the Fig. 2C, Tb-GMP/ATA CPs and Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs had no obvious diffraction peaks, indicating an amorphous structure. FT-IR spectra were recorded to further validate the interaction in CPs. For Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs, the two peaks at 3506 and 3392 cm−1 attributing to N–H bond of ATA disappeared, and the C = O stretching vibration of carboxyl group at 1694 cm−1 experienced faintly blue shift, implying that both carboxyl and amino groups of ATA participated in the coordination (Fig. 2D). In addition, the IR peaks of GMP at 1675 cm−1 (C = O), 1624 cm−1 (N7-C8), 978 cm−1 (P-O) slightly shifted to 1686 cm−1, 1636 cm−1, 902 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 2E), suggesting successful coordination of GMP via P-O, C = O and N7-C8 [33,34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Laccase-like activity of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs and their response toward DA
ce:section.ce:para: It has been demonstrated that GMP-Cu CPs own laccase-mimicking activity which can catalyze the typical substrate (2, 4-dichlorophenol) 2, 4-DP oxidation and result in a red adduct in the presence of (4-aminoantipyrine) 4-AP [27]. Therefore, we verified the feasibility of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs as laccase mimics via this chromogenic reaction. From Fig. 3 B, 2, 4-DP, 4-AP, and their mixture did not show any absorption in the range of 400 to 650 nm. In the presence of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs, the colorless mixture of 2, 4-DP and 4-AP became red, and a distinct absorption peak appeared at around 510 nm. As shown, Cu2+ could also initiate this color reaction due to its inner laccase-like activity. Obviously, the Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs displayed superior laccase-like activity because of the much deeper red color and larger absorption compared with that of Cu2+ system (Fig. 3B).

As a catecholamine neurotransmitter, DA can be catalyzed oxidation via laccase or its mimics in the presence of O2 to produce polydopamine (PDA) [35,36]. Similarly, in the presence of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs, DA could also be oxidized to black PDA with a broad absorption peak centered at 475 nm (Fig. 3C), consistent with the previous report [37]. The PDA possessed a broad absorption range from 400 to 600 nm, which was overlapped well with the fluorescence of the probes. Therefore, it was speculated that the oxidation product PDA might quench the luminescence of the probes due to the internal filtering effect.

Consequently, we examined the response of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs toward DA. As expected, when DA was added, the fluorescence of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs decreased distinctly (Fig. 4 A). While for Tb-GMP/ATA CPs, there was almost no change in luminescence upon DA, implying that the doped Cu2+ played an irreplaceable role for the response toward DA. Intriguingly, Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs exhibited an obvious bigger signal quenching compared to that of free Cu2+ in the presence of DA (Fig. 4B). This attributed to the enhanced catalytic ability of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs, which may owing to the porous structure of CPs, as well as the high local concentration of Cu2+ in the CPs [38].

The response of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs toward DA made it possible for constructing a ratiometric sensor. To improve detection properties, we optimized the conditions that may affect sensor performance, including excitation wavelength, ratio of Tb3+ to Cu2+ in Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs, buffer types, pH value of buffer solution, and incubation time. Firstly, the excitation wavelength was optimized. The optimal excitation wavelength for ATA and Tb-GMP CPs were 339 nm and 289 nm, respectively (Fig. 5 A). And the two luminescence centers could also be excited simultaneously under a single excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 5B, with increasing excitation wavelength in the range of 284–329 nm, the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ decreased, while that of ATA increased. Due to the fact that DA was detected mainly through the fluorescence quenching of Tb3+, the best excitation wavelength was identified as 289 nm. As well, the fluorescence intensity of ATA in this system was acceptable. The content of doped Cu2+, which could impact the fluorescence and catalytic activity of the Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs, was further optimized based on the fluorescence response toward DA. As shown in Fig. 5C, the initial fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ at 547 nm decreased as the content of Cu2+ rose, and the sensitivity of the CPs in response to DA was also degraded. Thus, the optimum molar ratio of Tb3+ to Cu2+ 10:1 was thus chosen for the following experiments. We then studied the effect of pH value from 5 to 10 on the fluorescence intensity of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs in the Tris-HCl buffer. The fluorescence intensity at 547 nm increased with increasing pH value, reaching a maximum at pH 8 and then decreasing thereafter (Fig. 5D). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+ quenched mostly at pH 8 in the presence of DA (Fig. 5E). Alarmingly, even at the same pH value, we found that different buffer solutions also affected the fluorescence intensity of the CPs, as well as the response upon DA. As shown in Fig. 5F, the Tris-HCl buffer solution at pH 8 was the best choice due to the strongest luminescence of CPs and the maximum response. Finally, the incubation time of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs and DA was examined. The ratio of fluorescence intensity at 427 nm and 547 nm (I427/I547) of the CPs decreased apparently at an initial period and gradually remained stable after 30 min (Fig. 5G). Thus, the following measurements were carried out after incubating with DA for 30 min.

Under the optimal conditions, the response of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs to different concentrations of DA at pH 8 were firstly investigated. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 A, when DA concentration increased continually, the fluorescence of Tb3+ gradually decreased, accompanied by a slight decrease in ATA luminescence. By plotting the fluorescence intensity ratio of I547/I427 versus DA concentration, it was found that the linear correlation formulas were as follows: when the DA concentration ranged from 1 to 10 μM, I547/I427 = 10.58–0.44C/μM (R2 = 0.992); when the concentration range was 10–400 μM, I547/I427 = 6.18–0.01C/μM (R2 = 0.997) (Fig. 6B), LOD: 0.44 μM (3σ, σ is standard deviation of blank solution). This had better or comparable performance than most previously reported optical methods for DA determination.

Although the Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs was most sensitive to DA at pH 8, it is more appropriate to detect biomolecules in physiological environments, so the detection performance of these probes was investigated at pH 7.4. Similar phenomena were achieved (Fig. 7 A), and a good linear relationship was found in the range of 100–1200 μΜ (Fig. 7B). The linear equation was I547/I427 = 11.18–0.005C/μM (R2 = 0.993), and the detection limit was 0.75 μM. This suggested that these dual-emission probes were also feasible to detect DA at 7.4.

The selectivity of the proposed Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs for DA detection was further evaluated. Some metal ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+) and some small biological molecules (NE, EP, Cys, ACH, ASP, Gly, GSH, Glu) were separately added to the sensing system. As shown in Fig. 8 , except DA, none of them caused distinct change of fluorescence intensity ratio of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs, revealing that the proposed assay for DA is highly selective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis and determination of DA in serum
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the practicability of Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs, serum DA levels were determined by spiking recovery experiments. As shown in the Table 1 , the recovery rate of DA in serum was 95.5 ∼ 104.9 %, and the relative standard deviation of three samples was not more than 2.1 %. The results showed that the sensor could be used for quantitative analysis of DA in serum samples with excellent reproducibility and recovery, which met the needs of practical analysis.

In order to investigate the accuracy of our method, we compared the obtained results from our method with that of standard HPLC method (Table 2 ) for the serum sample. As shown, the concentration of DA in serum was 5.2 μM based on our method, which was very close to that of HPLC method (5.3 μM). Besides, the proposed method exhibited comparative recovery and RSD with that of HPLC method for the spiked serum samples. Therefore, it could be concluded that the proposed method was accurate for the determination of DA in normal human serum samples.

In summary, Cu-doped Ln CPs were designed and prepared via facile self-assembly, which integrated the fluorescent and catalytic properties. The obtained Tb/Cu-GMP/ATA CPs not only emitted both fluorescence of ATA and Tb3+, but also possessed enhanced laccase-like activity. They could catalyze the oxidation of DA to PDA, which quenched the fluorescence of probes by internal filtration effect. Therefore, a ratiometric sensor for DA determination was constructed accordingly, which were satisfactorily applied for quantitative analysis of DA in HBS. With advantages of adjustable luminescence, simple synthesis, and low cost, Ln CPs are potential candidates for preparation of multi-functional probes for sensing application.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 22176179 and 42077389).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and synthesis of fluorescent reagents for selective detection of dopamine
Sens. Actuators B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Suzuki, Design and synthesis of fluorescent reagents for selective detection of dopamine, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 239 (2017) 383-389.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: UiO-66-NH2 MOF-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of dopamine and reduced glutathione
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Wang, M. Xie, M. Wang, Z. Li, X. Su, UiO-66-NH2 MOF-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of dopamine and reduced glutathione, Talanta, 220 (2020) 121352.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polymer dots as a novel probe for fluorescence sensing of dopamine and imaging in single living cell using droplet microfluidic platform
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Alizadeh, A. Salimi, Polymer dots as a novel probe for fluorescence sensing of dopamine and imaging in single living cell using droplet microfluidic platform, Analytica Chimica Acta, 1091 (2019) 40-49.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent trends in analytical approaches for detecting neurotransmitters in Alzheimer's disease
TrAC Trends Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Sangubotla, J. Kim, Recent trends in analytical approaches for detecting neurotransmitters in Alzheimer's disease, TrAC Trends in Analytical Chemistry, 105 (2018) 240-250.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence detection of dopamine based on the polyphenol oxidase–mimicking enzyme
Anal. Bioanal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Huang, J. Bai, J. Li, L. Lei, W. Zhang, S. Yan, Y. Li, Fluorescence detection of dopamine based on the polyphenol oxidase–mimicking enzyme, Analytical and Bioanalytical Chemistry, 412 (2020) 5291-5297.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multivalent Ce-MOFs as biomimetic laccase nanozyme for environmental remediation
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Liang, X. L. Wu, J. Xiong, X. Yuan, S. L. Liu, M. H. Zong, W. Y. Lou, Multivalent Ce-MOFs as biomimetic laccase nanozyme for environmental remediation, Chemical Engineering Journal, 450 (2022) 138220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chiral molecular imprinting-based SERS detection strategy for absolute enantiomeric discrimination
Nat. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Arabi, A. Ostovan, Y. Wang, R. Mei, L. Fu, J. Li, X. Wang, L. Chen, Chiral molecular imprinting-based SERS detection strategy for absolute enantiomeric discrimination, Nature Communications, 13 (2022)33448.
